CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought moderate to heavy snowfall to the North-Central Mountains as well as to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Storm totals included:  11.5 inches at the Tower Snotel, 8 inches; 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs, 7.5 inches; 3 miles west of Jamestown, 7 inches; 7 miles east-northeast of Red Feather Lakes at Roach Snotel, with 6 inches observed at Bear Lake National Park, 1 mile north of Bergen Park, 3 miles southwest of Conifer, Pinewood Lake and Winter Park.  Along the Urban Corridor there were some locally higher amounts reported near the foothills of Boulder and Larimer Counties.  Storm totals in those areas included:  7 inches; 3 miles south-southeast of Fort Collins with 6 inches observed in Boulder and at Horsetooth Inlet Bay.  Elsewhere across the Urban Corridor, storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

A low pressure system moved across the area bringing very strong surface winds and light to moderate snow.  Blizzard conditions were reported over eastern Colorado and parts of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the day. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions reported with visibility to 100 yards.

A major Spring storm system affecting the Central High Plains produced 16 tornadoes and flash flooding, in addition to severe hail and wind.  This was the most tornadoes reported in a single day in the Goodland County Warning Area.  The most significant tornado was an EF2 which began in Sherman county and moved north across most of Cheyenne county in Kansas.  Four homesteads suffered significant property damage along the 36 mile path length.  In addition to the EF2 tornado, there were three EF1 and twelve EF0 tornadoes stretching from Greeley county Kansas into Dundy county Nebraska.  No injuries or fatalities occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm began on November 30th and continued into December. Please see the November 2007 Storm Data publication for additional information on this episode. A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across western Colorado ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. This was a relatively warm system initially causing unseasonably high snow levels and mixed precipitation across much of the mountains. After colder air moved into the area moderate to heavy snow impacted some valleys and basins across western Colorado. Heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches blanketed the area above 8,500 feet MSL while amounts of 3 to 8 inches occurred below 8,500 feet. Frequent wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable snowpack resulted in avalanches in some of the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few avalanches came down onto Highway 550 between Red Mountain Pass and Silverton. The largest avalanche came down the East Riverside North slide path and put 3 feet of snow on the centerline of the road.  A vehicle got high-centered and stuck as the driver was trying to drive over the snow.  As the stuck driver got out of her vehicle, another avalanche came down and pinned the driver to the side of her vehicle.  The victim was rescued by a big truck driver, and the victim was apparently unhurt.  The Mother Cline slide path was one of the other locations where an avalanche ran onto Highway 550.  The avalanches closed Highway 550 for one hour and 25 minutes, from 0455 to 0620 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranging from 2 feet to nearly 5 feet occurred with frequent wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph. The snow resulted in closure of Highway 50 over Monarch Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 2 feet occurred with frequent wind gusts of 20 to 37 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of moist southwest to west flow ahead of a low pressure system resulted in heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains and high plateaus of western Colorado. Much of the lower elevations received significant snowfall as well. Precipitation began as rain across much of the area due to the relatively warm nature of the storm in the initial stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3.5 feet occurred with frequent wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph. Local gusts of 98 mph and 76 mph were recorded near 13000 feet at Eagle Mountain and Kendall Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwesterly airflow brought heavy snow to the higher elevations of the eastern San Juan Mountains, Sawatch Range, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  Snow accumulations were between 2 and 4 feet in the eastern San Juan Mountains...with the summit of Wolf Creek Pass picking up nearly four feet.  Around a foot of snow accumulated in the upper reaches of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains...and one to two feet fell in the Sawatch Range.
An upper level disturbance and cold front moved across the area and produced significant snowfall across most of the mountains and many valleys of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 4 inches occurred with numerous traffic accidents.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SAN JUAN MOUNTAINS ABOVE 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the Southwest U.S. produced up to 10 inches of snow.  Some of the higher snow totals include...6 inches near Fort Garland in Costilla County...and northwest of Buena Vista in Chaffee County...8 inches at Cuchara in Huerfano County and southwest of South Fork in Mineral County.  Snow amounts exceeded Winter Storm criterion in the eastern San Juan Mountains...where 10 inches occurred at the Wolf Creek Ski Area in Mineral County.

High winds developed in and near the Front Range Foothills of Boulder and Jefferson Counties.  Peak wind gusts included:  89 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 88 mph atop Niwot Ridge; 83 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Bergen Park; 80 mph, 3 miles southeast of Jamestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought moderate to heavy snow to the North-Central Mountains of Colorado.  Snow amounts generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with heavier amounts along the eastern slopes of the Park and northern Gore Ranges.  Storm totals for specific locations included:  18 inches at Tower Snotel; 16 inches at Copper Mountain Ski Area; 12 inches at Jones Pass; 10.5 inches at Winter Park Ski Area; 10 inches at Roach Snotel; 9.5 inches at Columbine Snotel; 9 inches at Joe Wright; 8.5 inches at Michigan Creek Snotel; 8 inches at Summit Ranch.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier was buried by an avalanche; the incident occurred near Zimmerman Lake and Montgomery Pass Trail, west of Fort Collins. The skier's friends found him and dug him out, estimating that he was buried for about 10 minutes before they freed him.  He was buried under 3 feet of snow.  They performed CPR on him and had him breathing when rescuers arrived.  The man died after being in a coma for several days.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a moist zonal flow and strong upper level jet stream brought extensive blowing and drifting snow to the North-Central Mountains of Colorado.  Wind sensors above timberline measured peak gusts from 75 to 92 mph.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in whiteout conditions and a high avalanche danger along the Interstate 70 Corridor, from Floyd Hill to Vail.  As a result, the interstate was closed for approximately 24 hours.  Other closures included:  Berthoud and Rabbit Ears Passes on U.S. Highway 40; Loveland Pass on U.S. Highway 6; Cameron Pass on State Highway 14; Kenosha Pass on U.S. Highway 285; U.S. Highways 287 and 85 near the Wyoming Border.   ||The American Red Cross opened 9 shelters in Summit County for stranded motorists; where more than 2,000 people took shelter.  Loveland Ski Area was forced to close for the day because employees and skiers couldn't get to it.  Tow truck drivers spent much of their time rescuing stranded motorists.  In Grand County, a snowmobiler was injured when he triggered an avalanche.  He was knocked unconscious and buried under 3 feet of snow.  Fortunately, he suffered only a separated shoulder.  ||Peak wind gust reports included:  92 mph at Niwot Ridge; 89 mph at Mines peak; 87 mph at Georgetown; 80 mph at Berthoud and Loveland Passes; 75 mph at Gunsight; 60 mph at Copper Mountain.  Storm totals during the 2-day period were light to moderate with amounts ranging from 5 to 10 inches.  The exception occurred at the Columbine Snotel where 15 inches of snowfall was measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow and wind resulted in whiteout conditions and a high avalanche danger along the Interstate 70 Corridor, from Floyd Hill to Vail. As a result, the interstate was closed for approximately 24 hours. Others closures included: Loveland Pass on U.S. Highway 6, Kenosha Pass on U.S. Highway 285, and Berthoud Pass on U.S. Highway 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across western Colorado ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. This was a relatively warm system initially causing unseasonably high snow levels and mixed precipitation across much of the mountains. After colder air moved into the area moderate to heavy snow impacted some valleys and basins across western Colorado. Heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations. This storm continued into the month of December. For a continuation on this episode please see the December 2007 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches blanketed the area above the 8,500 foot level, while amounts of 3 to 8 inches were measured below 8,500 feet. Frequent wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow. For a continuation on this event please see the December 2007 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of moist southwest to west flow ahead of a low pressure system resulted in heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains and high plateaus of western Colorado. Much of the lower elevations received significant snowfall as well. Precipitation began as rain across much of the area due to the relatively warm nature of the storm in the initial stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches occurred with wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph. Highway 139 over Douglas Pass received 10 inches.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong damaging winds developed in the North-Central Mountains.  Peak wind reports included 85 mph, 9 miles west-southwest of Rustic, and 79 mph atop Mines Peak and Niwot Ridge.  A 68-year-old hiker was killed, and his friend injured, when a tree fell on them in Rocky Mountain National Park. He was struck by a falling tree about a mile and a half from the trailhead at the entrance of the Wild Basin Area. The dead 50-ft tall tree was a Douglas fir. The top broke off 15 feet above the ground and glanced off three standing trees before landing on the trail. The tree was approximately 10 inches in diameter at the point it hit the hikers.

CZ_NAME: ELBERT / C & E DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems, one moving to the south and east of the region, and the other brushing from the west, contributed to heavy snow along the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  The heaviest snow fell south and east of Denver where a blizzard developed during the late morning and early afternoon hours. In and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide, storm totals ranged from 6 to 15 inches.  Across the Northeast Plains, storm totals ranged from 2 to 11 inches.  |Very strong winds, produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along the Interstate 70 corridor, from just east of Denver to near Limon. Sustained winds from 30 to 45 mph were measured near Cedar Point with peak gusts to 60 mph.  As a result, snow drifts 2 to 4 feet in depth made some roads impassable with whiteout conditions reported.  Interstate 70, from Airpark Road to Burlington, and State Highway 86, near Cedar Point, were closed due to very poor driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds from 20 to 30 mph, strongest east of Interstate 25,  combined with heavy snow to produce near blizzard conditions along the Palmer Divide.  As a result, areas of blowing snow produced drifts up to 4 feet deep.  Storm totals included:  15 inches, 4 miles east of Parker, 13 inches, 5 miles south-southwest of Arapahoe Park, 12.5 inches, 4 miles south-southwest of Elizabeth, with 9 inches, around Castle Rock, Parker and Sedalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche swept two vehicles off State Highway 40, near Berthoud Pass, on the way to Winter Park.  The slide covered all three lanes of the highway.  The vehicles were covered and swept off the roadway.  Eight people were in the vehicles, but only one person was injured.  He suffered a broken rib.  The slide was approximately 200 feet wide and 15 feet deep.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the Front Range of Colorado on Christmas day.  The heaviest snow fell in and near the foothills of Boulder, Douglas and Jefferson Counties.  The snow triggered accidents throughout Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area.  Gusty winds stirred snow drifts from 2 to 3.5 feet in depth.  Officially, the total calendar day snowfall for Denver was 7.8 inches, setting the record for Christmas Day.  The previous record was 6.2 inches, established in 1894.  ||Storm totals in the Front Range Foothills:  13.5 inches at Coal Creek Canyon, Genesee and 3 miles east-southeast of Tiny Town; 12.5 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Conifer; 12 inches, 5 miles east-southeast of Aspen Park; 11 inches, 6 miles southwest of Kassler; 10.5 inches at Eldorado Springs.  Elsewhere, storm totals range from with 5 to 10 inches.||In the Urban Corridor storm totals included:  9 inches near Elizabeth; 8 inches in southwest Denver, Highlands Ranch, Marston Reservoir and Wheat Ridge; 7.5 inches in Arvada; 7 inches in Centennial and Lakewood; 6.5 inches in Aurora and 8 miles southeast of Watkins; 6 inches in Boulder, Englewood and Parker.  Elsewhere, snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 6 to 14 inches.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with an intense upper level jet, and a very strong surface pressure gradient, developed in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 77 mph to 115 mph.  The strong winds coupled with freshly fallen snow resulted in whiteout conditions and several highway closures due to blowing and drifting snow.  Road closures included:  State Highway 93, between Golden and Boulder; State Highway 128, from Wadsworth Boulevard to State Highway 93; State Highway 36, from the Boulder Turnpike, in Broomfield, to South Boulder Road; State Highway 287, from Ft Collins to the Wyoming state line; State Highway 74, in Evergreen, between County Road 65 and Lewis Ridge Road. |More than 100 people were stranded in their cars between Golden and Boulder as blowing and drifting snow made the highway impassable.  Snow drifts along State Highway 93 were over 6 feet in depth.  As a result, the American Red Cross opened a shelter at Arvada West High School for the stranded commuters.  Up to twenty cars were also abandoned along the Diagonal Highway, between Boulder and Longmont.  Thirty vehicles were stranded along State Highway 128.  The high winds also caused intermittent power outages in Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 89 mph was recorded in Boulder County atop Niwot Ridge, and 3 miles northeast of Arapahoe Peak.  |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving winter storm across the Four-Corners region generated impressive mountain snows.  Some of the higher amounts reported include...9 to 11 inches at Rosita and San Isabel in Custer County...Rye in Pueblo County...and La Veta in Huerfano County...and the summit of Wolf Creek Pass and Wolf Creek Ski Area reported up to 45 inches of snow.  The snowfall over the eastern mountains was heavy...but occurred over a long period of time...so a warning was not necessary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with an intense upper level jet, and a very strong surface pressure gradient, developed in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 77 mph to 115 mph.  The strong winds coupled with freshly fallen snow resulted in whiteout conditions and several highway closures due to blowing and drifting snow.  Road closures included:  State Highway 93, between Golden and Boulder; State Highway 128, from Wadsworth Boulevard to State Highway 93; State Highway 36, from the Boulder Turnpike, in Broomfield, to South Boulder Road; State Highway 287, from Ft Collins to the Wyoming state line; State Highway 74, in Evergreen, between County Road 65 and Lewis Ridge Road. |More than 100 people were stranded in their cars between Golden and Boulder as blowing and drifting snow made the highway impassable.  Snow drifts along State Highway 93 were over 6 feet in depth.  As a result, the American Red Cross opened a shelter at Arvada West High School for the stranded commuters.  Up to twenty cars were also abandoned along the Diagonal Highway, between Boulder and Longmont.  Thirty vehicles were stranded along State Highway 128.  The high winds also caused intermittent power outages in Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  115 mph at the National Wind Technology Center near Eldorado Springs, 89 mph; 7 miles west-northwest of Berthoud, 78 mph at Lafayette, with 77 mph; 3 miles west-southwest of Boulder.   |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the Front Range of Colorado on Christmas day.  The heaviest snow fell in and near the foothills of Boulder, Douglas and Jefferson Counties.  The snow triggered accidents throughout Denver and the surrounding metropolitan area.  Gusty winds stirred snow drifts from 2 to 3.5 feet in depth.  Officially, the total calendar day snowfall for Denver was 7.8 inches, setting the record for Christmas Day.  The previous record was 6.2 inches, established in 1894.  ||Storm totals in the Front Range Foothills:  13.5 inches at Coal Creek Canyon, Genesee and 3 miles east-southeast of Tiny Town; 12.5 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Conifer; 12 inches, 5 miles east-southeast of Aspen Park; 11 inches, 6 miles southwest of Kassler; 10.5 inches at Eldorado Springs.  Elsewhere, storm totals range from with 5 to 10 inches.||In the Urban Corridor storm totals included:  9 inches near Elizabeth; 8 inches in southwest Denver, Highlands Ranch, Marston Reservoir and Wheat Ridge; 7.5 inches in Arvada; 7 inches in Centennial and Lakewood; 6.5 inches in Aurora and 8 miles southeast of Watkins; 6 inches in Boulder, Englewood and Parker.  Elsewhere, snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches.  Locally higher amounts from 8 to 11 inches were observed in the foothills of southern Boulder County.

A fast moving winter storm caused whiteout conditions to form over the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  Interstate 70 was closed from just east of Denver; from Airpark Road, to the Kansas border.  Snow drifts up to 3 feet deep were reported. Prior to the closure of Interstate 70, the icy conditions and zero visibilities caused a 35-car pileup, just east of Denver near E-470. Storm totals ranged from 1 inch to around 6.5 inches of snow, heaviest along the Palmer Divide south and east of Denver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream over northern Colorado, coupled with a passing weather disturbance, brought a one-two punch of heavy snow and strong winds to areas in and near the Front Range.  In the North Central Mountains, a peak wind gust to 106 mph was recorded atop Berthoud Pass.  U.S. Highway 40 was closed temporarily at Berthoud Pass, and between Kremmling and Steamboat Springs, due to an increased avalanche danger.  One avalanche knocked a state highway vehicle off the road near Berthoud Pass.  At Winter Park, the ski area was forced to shut down when strong winds toppled several trees.  Elsewhere, two sections of Interstate 70 were closed including a 60-mile stretch from east of Vail to west of Evergreen.  U.S. Highway 6 over Loveland Pass, and U.S. Highway 285, over Kenosha Pass, were also closed.  The American Red Cross set up temporary shelters in Granby, Fraser and Silverthorne for stranded travelers.  Storm totals in the high country ranged from 7 to 18 inches.|Very intense winds also developed in the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  At the National Wind Technology Center, the peak wind gust topped out at 101 mph.  In and near the Front Range Foothills, the wind stirred up intense ground blizzards which resulted in several road closures.  State Highway 93, between Golden and Boulder was closed for much of the day.  In addition, State Highways 72 and 128, from Indiana St. and McCaslin Blvd. respectively, were closed due to near zero visibilities in blowing and drifting snow.  East of Interstate 25, peak wind gusts to around 60 mph were recorded.  At Denver International Airport, blowing debris cracked the front and side windshields of 14 airplanes within a three hour period.  In all, three airlines were impacted in the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Front Range Foothills, peak wind gusts included 92 mph at Carter Lake, and 86 mph at Georgetown.  Elsewhere, peak gusts ranged from 65 to 75 mph.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream over northern Colorado, coupled with a passing weather disturbance, brought a one-two punch of heavy snow and strong winds to areas in and near the Front Range.  In the North Central Mountains, a peak wind gust to 106 mph was recorded atop Berthoud Pass.  U.S. Highway 40 was closed temporarily at Berthoud Pass, and between Kremmling and Steamboat Springs, due to an increased avalanche danger.  One avalanche knocked a state highway vehicle off the road near Berthoud Pass.  At Winter Park, the ski area was forced to shut down when strong winds toppled several trees.  Elsewhere, two sections of Interstate 70 were closed including a 60-mile stretch from east of Vail to west of Evergreen.  U.S. Highway 6 over Loveland Pass, and U.S. Highway 285, over Kenosha Pass, were also closed.  The American Red Cross set up temporary shelters in Granby, Fraser and Silverthorne for stranded travelers.  Storm totals in the high country ranged from 7 to 18 inches.|Very intense winds also developed in the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  At the National Wind Technology Center, the peak wind gust topped out at 101 mph.  In and near the Front Range Foothills, the wind stirred up intense ground blizzards which resulted in several road closures.  State Highway 93, between Golden and Boulder was closed for much of the day.  In addition, State Highways 72 and 128, from Indiana St. and McCaslin Blvd. respectively, were closed due to near zero visibilities in blowing and drifting snow.  East of Interstate 25, peak wind gusts to around 60 mph were recorded.  At Denver International Airport, blowing debris cracked the front and side windshields of 14 airplanes within a three hour period.  In all, three airlines were impacted in the incident.

A storm system wound itself up over Southeast Colorado, allowing for heavy snow and rain to develop over much of North-Central and Northeast Colorado.  Severe thunderstorms preceded the storm system on the 23rd, affecting the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Severe weather included:  large hail; up to golfball size, damaging thunderstorm winds and several small tornadoes.  |On the 24th, heavy snow fell in the foothills west of Denver and Boulder, where storm totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Heavy snow also occurred along the Palmer Divide, with storm totals from 10 to 16 inches.  Elsewhere, a steady moderate to heavy rainfall was reported.  Denver International Airport measured 1.64 inches of precipitation, which broke the single day record for the 24th of April.  A few short lived tornadoes also developed over Lincoln County, in the vicinity of the surface cyclone.  |The heavy wet snow caused several power outages.  In some instances, it took several days to restore power.  Several road closures were reported, including Interstates 25 and 70.  A jackknifed semi-trailer backed up traffic for nearly 20 miles; on southbound I-25, between Denver and Colorado Springs.  In addition, a 50-ton boulder blocked the southbound lane of State Highway 285, near Parmalee Gulch.  Crews had to use explosives to break up the boulder and clear the debris.  Stranded buses and impassable roadways also forced several school closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copius amounts of moisture and high winds associated with a low pressure system caused an increase in avalanche danger. This led to some natural avalanches and resulted in mitigation procedures that produced additional avalanches in the western San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On January 12th, 2 natural avalanches near Red Mountain Pass deposited snow on Highway 550 for a distance of 36 feet at a depth of 2 feet on the centerline. Avalanche mitigation procedures resulted in a number of man-made slides which also deposited debris on roadways near Red Mountain Pass, Coal Bank Pass, and Molas Pass. Periodic road closures occurred for a total of 41 hours and 40 minutes at Red Mountain Pass, and for 8 hours and 25 minutes for Coal Bank and Molas Passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typical Colorado March weather was observed on the 23rd and 24th, as a storm system moved from Arizona and ejected across Northeast Colorado.  Severe thunderstorms preceded this storm system as golfball size hail fell north of Fort Morgan on the 23rd.  Locally heavy snow developed in the North-Central Mountains.  Storm totals included:  11 inches at Joe Wright, 10.5 inches at Never Summer, and 10 inches at Eldora Ski Area and Winter Park.|As the heavy snow fell in the North-Central Mountains; severe thunderstorms producing tornadoes developed across Northeast Colorado during the early evening hours of the 24th.  The severe thunderstorms produced multi-vortex tornadoes which developed over Phillips and Sedgwick Counties.  The tornadoes, however, were short lived and weak as they skipped around the area.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down but did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms occurred over extreme southeast Colorado... producing damaging winds and large hail. One supercell generated large hail...damaging winds...and two tornadoes.  The tornado which struck Holly...rural northeast Prowers County...and rural southeast Kiowa County had a maximum rating of EF3.  The path width reached a maximum of around 900 yards in extreme northeast Prowers County.  Prior to this...as the tornado moved through Holly...the path width was around 600 feet.  Over 200 residences and other buildings were affected or destroyed.  Two people were killed and nine others were injured. The damage path was around 28 miles long...extending into Kiowa County.  The last substantial damage with the tornado was 12 miles north of Holly...in northeast Prowers County...where a ranch sustained high end EF3 damage.  As the supercell cycled through...another tornado occurred east of Towner...and moved into Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With a maximum rating of EF3 and a maximum damage path width of 900 yards, the tornado raced through Holly, causing two fatalities and nine injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major Spring storm system affecting the Central High Plains produced 16 tornadoes and flash flooding, in addition to severe hail and wind.  This was the most tornadoes reported in a single day in the Goodland County Warning Area.  The most significant tornado was an EF2 which began in Sherman county and moved north across most of Cheyenne county in Kansas.  Four homesteads suffered significant property damage along the 36 mile path length.  In addition to the EF2 tornado, there were three EF1 and twelve EF0 tornadoes stretching from Greeley county Kansas into Dundy county Nebraska.  No injuries or fatalities occurred.

A few severe thunderstorms occurred over extreme southeast Colorado... producing damaging winds and large hail. One supercell generated large hail...damaging winds...and two tornadoes.  The tornado which struck Holly...rural northeast Prowers County...and rural southeast Kiowa County had a maximum rating of EF3.  The path width reached a maximum of around 900 yards in extreme northeast Prowers County.  Prior to this...as the tornado moved through Holly...the path width was around 600 feet.  Over 200 residences and other buildings were affected or destroyed.  Two people were killed and nine others were injured. The damage path was around 28 miles long...extending into Kiowa County.  The last substantial damage with the tornado was 12 miles north of Holly...in northeast Prowers County...where a ranch sustained high end EF3 damage.  As the supercell cycled through...another tornado occurred east of Towner...and moved into Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A California closed low merged with a northwest cold front which produced several inches of new snow. This caused an unstable snowpack and several avalanches across portions of the San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three natural avalanches covered a portion of Highway 550 near Red Mountain Pass, covering the roadway with up to four feet of snow for a total length of 161 feet along the centerline. CDOT initiated several avalanches in the area for mitigation purposes. Periodic road closures occurred while debris was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary and unstable atmosphere generated severe thunderstorms with large hail, damaging winds, and tornados.  All the reported severe weather occurred in Prowers and Baca Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two supercells occurred in central and northern Prowers County between 4:20 p.m. and 5:40 p.m.  At approximately 4:30 p.m., a funnel cloud was reported several miles southwest of Granada.  At 5:25 p.m. a large, slow moving, slowly rotating tornado moving approximately three miles between ranch houses southwest of Granada.  There was no known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved across the Great Basin. A deep and moist southerly flow ahead of the system resulted in heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains and high plateau areas of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This late season storm produced around 11 inches of snow across the Roan and Tavaputs Plateaus. In addition to the snow, frequent wind gusts of 30 to 45 mph buffeted the area and caused blowing and drifting snow. A wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at Douglas Pass.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold spring-time airmass resulted in freezing temperatures across some of the lower valleys of west central and southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 28 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable snowpack resulted in avalanches in some of the mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people were on a backcountry ski vacation near Aspen. They chose to ascend Mount Shimer about 7 miles southeast of Aspen. They were ascending a tree covered, north facing slope near the 12,088 foot level. At approximately 1:50 pm, the lead skier remotely triggered a large avalanche in the bowl to the east. Just after this, a smaller avalanche fractured above the party. The avalanche caught all three, but the upper skier was only carried for approximately 100 feet. The avalanche swept the two lower men on split snowboards down the slope and buried both. The victims were buried, one above the other with about 100 feet between them. The upper skier found his two companions and uncovered debris from their heads and notified rescue personnel. When rescue personnel arrived on the scene, they determined that both victims were dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system wound itself up over Southeast Colorado, allowing for heavy snow and rain to develop over much of North-Central and Northeast Colorado.  Severe thunderstorms preceded the storm system on the 23rd, affecting the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Severe weather included:  large hail; up to golfball size, damaging thunderstorm winds and several small tornadoes.  |On the 24th, heavy snow fell in the foothills west of Denver and Boulder, where storm totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Heavy snow also occurred along the Palmer Divide, with storm totals from 10 to 16 inches.  Elsewhere, a steady moderate to heavy rainfall was reported.  Denver International Airport measured 1.64 inches of precipitation, which broke the single day record for the 24th of April.  A few short lived tornadoes also developed over Lincoln County, in the vicinity of the surface cyclone.  |The heavy wet snow caused several power outages.  In some instances, it took several days to restore power.  Several road closures were reported, including Interstates 25 and 70.  A jackknifed semi-trailer backed up traffic for nearly 20 miles; on southbound I-25, between Denver and Colorado Springs.  In addition, a 50-ton boulder blocked the southbound lane of State Highway 285, near Parmalee Gulch.  Crews had to use explosives to break up the boulder and clear the debris.  Stranded buses and impassable roadways also forced several school closures.

A major Spring storm system affecting the Central High Plains produced 16 tornadoes and flash flooding, in addition to severe hail and wind.  This was the most tornadoes reported in a single day in the Goodland County Warning Area.  The most significant tornado was an EF2 which began in Sherman county and moved north across most of Cheyenne county in Kansas.  Four homesteads suffered significant property damage along the 36 mile path length.  In addition to the EF2 tornado, there were three EF1 and twelve EF0 tornadoes stretching from Greeley county Kansas into Dundy county Nebraska.  No injuries or fatalities occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over open fields...no known damage.

In addition, a 50-ton boulder blocked the southbound lane of State Highway 285, near Parmalee Gulch.  Crews had to use explosives to break up the boulder and clear the debris.  Stranded buses and impassable roadways also forced several school closures.

A slow moving pacific storm system, ejecting from the Desert Southwest, brought a period of unsettled weather to Northeast Colorado.  During the 3-day period from May 3rd to May 5th, locally heavy snow was reported over parts of the Front Range Foothills.  Storm totals included: 15 inches near Conifer, 14.5 inches west of Jamestown, 13.5 inches; 6 miles southwest of Evergreen, and 12.5 inches at Pine Junction.  Severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and several small short-lived tornadoes, occurred during the period as well.  No damage was reported with any of the tornadoes.  ||In addition, lightning struck a residence in Jefferson County.  The roof of the house was hit, causing the attic to catch on fire.  The fire was confined to the one room; there were no injuries.  At Denver International Airport, lightning also struck a United Airlines jet as it was pushing away from the gate.  The passengers were taken off the jet and put on another plane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail, very heavy rain and tornadoes impacted the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains.  Heavy rain associated with a severe thunderstorm caused flooding along a small tributary draining into the South Platte River.  The incident occurred at 13th and Decatur Street, near Invesco Field.  The floodwaters inundated the bike path adjacent to the creek.  A woman with her child sought refuge under a bridge and became trapped by the high water.  The woman slipped and the stroller containing the child was swept into the swift current.  Hail up to quarter size was reported over much of the Urban Corriodor.  The exception was near Hudson, where golfball size hail was observed.

Slow moving thunderstorms developed across Cheyenne county in eastern Colorado during the late evening hours on the 27th and continued overnight.  Doppler radar estimated three inches of rain fell.  Heavy rainfall resulted in one railroad bridge wash out and elevated levels on small streams and creeks throughout the county, with water running across country roads and farmland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eureka Creek railroad bridge, just north of Hwy 40 about 6 miles ENE of Kit Carson, washed out. Water was also reported flowing over Highway 40. Law enforcement also reported flooding of county road 27 (south of U.S. highway 40) in this same area.  Road 43 north of Cheyenne Wells was also reported to be covered with water from this same heavy rain event.

Lightning sparked a fire at a Boulder home and caused a fuel tank in a farm field to explode, near Teller Farm Open Space on Valmont Road.    Lightning struck the roof of the residence, causing the rafters in the attic to catch fire.  Damage to the home was estimated to be $5000.  The 15,000-gallon fuel tank, which stored diesel gas for farm equipment, was also struck.  The explosion shot the tank an estimated 150 feet in the air and it landed approximately 400 feet from its original location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving pacific storm system, ejecting from the Desert Southwest, brought a period of unsettled weather to Northeast Colorado.  During the 3-day period from May 3rd to May 5th, locally heavy snow was reported over parts of the Front Range Foothills.  Storm totals included: 15 inches near Conifer, 14.5 inches west of Jamestown, 13.5 inches; 6 miles southwest of Evergreen, and 12.5 inches at Pine Junction.  Severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and several small short-lived tornadoes, occurred during the period as well.  No damage was reported with any of the tornadoes.  ||In addition, lightning struck a residence in Jefferson County.  The roof of the house was hit, causing the attic to catch on fire.  The fire was confined to the one room; there were no injuries.  At Denver International Airport, lightning also struck a United Airlines jet as it was pushing away from the gate.  The passengers were taken off the jet and put on another plane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a United Airlines jet as it departed the gate at Denver International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system and surface cold front in eastern Colorado resulted in a line of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Several reports of hail and a brief tornado occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado produced damage to outbuildings at a farmstead on Road 37.5 and Road LL in extreme northern Kit Carson county. A 25x100 shed was lofted and flipped over.  This resulted in damage to a hay bailer and two grain bins.

Severe thunderstorms, producing hail up to quarter size, and heavy rain; trained across northern portions of the Denver Metropolitan area.  The path of the storms stretched from around Golden to Denver International Airport.  The thunderstorms formed quickly following the passage of a cold front.  ||Some minor street flooding was also reported as hail clogged storm drains.  The hail piled up 4 inches deep at Elitch Gardens amusement park in downtown Denver.  Drivers were caught in up to 3 feet of water in some intersections.  Xcel reported scattered power failures around the Denver metropolitan area. At D.I.A. some brief delays occurred as airlines were forced to pull in their ground crews for safety reasons.  Lightning also struck two homes in Ft. Collins.  No one was hurt in either incident.  The incliment conditions forced the closure of Trail Ridge Road, in Rocky Mountain National Park, due to snow and very poor visibility.||Severe thunderstorms developed further south along the Palmer Divide. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter was reported over southern Elbert County.  Hail damage was reported in the towns of Elbert and Limon, where skylights and windows were broken.  Two tornadoes touched down but no structures were damaged.

CZ_NAME: SAGUACHE COUNTY EAST OF CONTINENTAL DIVIDE BELOW 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system generated widespread high winds over southern Colorado.  The damaging winds began during the last morning across the San Luis Valley and surrounding mountains.  Wind gusts up to 75 mph occurred, with widespread tree damage and damage to fences and outbuildings.  Around 2 p.m. MDT, a 69 kilovolt line between Antonito and Romeo, in the southern San Luis Valley, was taken down, and nearly 3000 people were without power for a time.  The high winds also started a wildfire at the Great Sand Dunes early in the afternoon.  Farther east, the most extensive damage occurred in the Wet Mountains in Custer County.  Forest Road 369 was closed due to a vast blow down of mature pine and spruce trees.  Hundreds of trees, some 100 feet tall and 200 years old, were brought down in a 4-mile swath.  Other minor damage occurred throughout the southeast plains into the early evening.  Another secondary surge of high winds occurred in the Pikes Peak Region after 3 a.m. MDT.  A tree fell on a business, and caused extensive damage to the air conditioning unit on the roof.

A strong weather system generated widespread high winds over southern Colorado.  The damaging winds began during the last morning across the San Luis Valley and surrounding mountains.  Wind gusts up to 75 mph occurred, with widespread tree damage and damage to fences and outbuildings.  Around 2 p.m. MDT, a 69 kilovolt line between Antonito and Romeo, in the southern San Luis Valley, was taken down, and nearly 3000 people were without power for a time.  The high winds also started a wildfire at the Great Sand Dunes early in the afternoon.  Farther east, the most extensive damage occurred in the Wet Mountains in Custer County.  Forest Road 369 was closed due to a vast blow down of mature pine and spruce trees.  Hundreds of trees, some 100 feet tall and 200 years old, were brought down in a 4-mile swath.  Other minor damage occurred throughout the southeast plains into the early evening.  Another secondary surge of high winds occurred in the Pikes Peak Region after 3 a.m. MDT.  A tree fell on a business, and caused extensive damage to the air conditioning unit on the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 6th, high winds in the San Luis Valley brough down an electrical transmission line which sparked a wildfire that eventually burned hundreds of acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds across the San Luis Valley knocked over an electrical transmission line on Lane 6, 10 miles southwest of the Great Sand Dunes.  The fire eventually consumed 320 acres of grass and brush as it spread to the northeast.  No structures were involved, but the Great Sand Dunes was evacuated until 5 p.m.

Around 2 p.m. MDT, a 69 kilovolt line between Antonito and Romeo, in the southern San Luis Valley, was taken down, and nearly 3000 people were without power for a time.  The high winds also started a wildfire at the Great Sand Dunes early in the afternoon.  Farther east, the most extensive damage occurred in the Wet Mountains in Custer County.  Forest Road 369 was closed due to a vast blow down of mature pine and spruce trees.  Hundreds of trees, some 100 feet tall and 200 years old, were brought down in a 4-mile swath.  Other minor damage occurred throughout the southeast plains into the early evening.  Another secondary surge of high winds occurred in the Pikes Peak Region after 3 a.m. MDT.  A tree fell on a business, and caused extensive damage to the air conditioning unit on the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwest flow ahead of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph buffeted the area.  Locally stronger gusts were measured to 80 mph at the Storm Peak Lab and 70 mph at Hangman RAWS.  The strong winds blew down some trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high-based thunderstorm resulted in a severe thunderstorm wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high based thunderstorm produced strong outflow winds that combined with strong gradient winds to produce the 92 mph wind gust.  The strong winds were observed by contract FAA air traffic controllers at the Eagle County Regional Airport tower.  Little or no damage occurred, as all aircraft were adequately secured when the strong winds struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind driven wildfire in hot and dry conditions swept across three homes in the community of Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire which started near the town of Mesa became wind driven in hot and dry conditions and destroyed three houses. Flames were reported to be 50 feet high with the blaze. About 30 acres were charred by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wildfire west of the Glenwood Springs area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning sparked wildfire about 8 miles west of Glenwood Springs burned about 1,240 acres consisting mainly of chaparral. The fire threatened up to 200 homes and resulted in temporary evacuations of many residences. The estimated cost of suppressing the fire was 1.1 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked the Whittaker Flats Fire near Interstate 70 in the Debeque Canyon area of Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked the Whittaker Flats Wildfire about 11 miles east of Grand Junction near Cameo on the Roan Plateau. The fire consumed about 1,016 acres of grass, sagebrush, and pinyon pine. The estimated cost of suppressing the blaze was 302 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system allowed for heavy rain to fall over parts of Northeastern Colorado, affecting primarily Sedgwick and Phillips Counties. Severe thunderstorms produce large hail and very heavy rain.  Downed power lines were reported over parts of Sedgwick County on the 11th.  Farmers in the area reported extensive flooding over southeastern Sedwick County from around Sixteen-mile Corner east to the Nebraska state line.  Several basements were also flooded. ||In addition, extensive flooding and flash flooding forced dozens of road closures in northeast Phillips County.  Colorado Highway 23; east of Amherst, and U.S. Highway 6; east of Holyoke, were closed due to flooding.  Dozens of secondary roads were also reportedly under water.  A section of railroad track about 1.5 miles southwest of Amherst was washed out with up to 10 feet of water covering the highway.  Extensive field flooding was also observed.  In a two day period, a trained spotter in eastern Phillips County measured over 10.3 inches of rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph occurred with reports of trees blown down and power outages. A tree was blown down over an integral transmission line near Hesperus causing the 115kV line to trip off line. Another smaller outage took place around 9 pm in the Skyline Ranch area when the strong wind took down a single phase line.

Farmers in the area reported extensive flooding over southeastern Sedwick County from around Sixteen-mile Corner east to the Nebraska state line.  Several basements were also flooded. ||In addition, extensive flooding and flash flooding forced dozens of road closures in northeast Phillips County.  Colorado Highway 23; east of Amherst, and U.S. Highway 6; east of Holyoke, were closed due to flooding.  Dozens of secondary roads were also reportedly under water.  A section of railroad track about 1.5 miles southwest of Amherst was washed out with up to 10 feet of water covering the highway.  Extensive field flooding was also observed.  In a two day period, a trained spotter in eastern Phillips County measured over 10.3 inches of rainfall.

This storm began on November 30th and continued into December. Please see the November 2007 Storm Data publication for additional information on this episode. A very moist subtropical flow of air spread across western Colorado ahead of a powerful low pressure system. This produced widespread heavy snow across the mountains along with strong winds. This was a relatively warm system initially causing unseasonably high snow levels and mixed precipitation across much of the mountains. After colder air moved into the area moderate to heavy snow impacted some valleys and basins across western Colorado. Heavy rainfall occurred across some of the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 40 inches blanketed the area while strong winds frequently gusting to 40 to 60 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting snow and white-out conditions. Ridgetop winds gusted up to 89 mph. Highway 550 was closed, blow down of trees occurred, and power was lost in Silverton.

A strong weather system generated widespread high winds over southern Colorado.  The damaging winds began during the last morning across the San Luis Valley and surrounding mountains.  Wind gusts up to 75 mph occurred, with widespread tree damage and damage to fences and outbuildings.  Around 2 p.m. MDT, a 69 kilovolt line between Antonito and Romeo, in the southern San Luis Valley, was taken down, and nearly 3000 people were without power for a time.  The high winds also started a wildfire at the Great Sand Dunes early in the afternoon.  Farther east, the most extensive damage occurred in the Wet Mountains in Custer County.  Forest Road 369 was closed due to a vast blow down of mature pine and spruce trees.  Hundreds of trees, some 100 feet tall and 200 years old, were brought down in a 4-mile swath.  Other minor damage occurred throughout the southeast plains into the early evening.  Another secondary surge of high winds occurred in the Pikes Peak Region after 3 a.m. MDT.  A tree fell on a business, and caused extensive damage to the air conditioning unit on the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system wound itself up over Southeast Colorado, allowing for heavy snow and rain to develop over much of North-Central and Northeast Colorado.  Severe thunderstorms preceded the storm system on the 23rd, affecting the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Severe weather included:  large hail; up to golfball size, damaging thunderstorm winds and several small tornadoes.  |On the 24th, heavy snow fell in the foothills west of Denver and Boulder, where storm totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Heavy snow also occurred along the Palmer Divide, with storm totals from 10 to 16 inches.  Elsewhere, a steady moderate to heavy rainfall was reported.  Denver International Airport measured 1.64 inches of precipitation, which broke the single day record for the 24th of April.  A few short lived tornadoes also developed over Lincoln County, in the vicinity of the surface cyclone.  |The heavy wet snow caused several power outages.  In some instances, it took several days to restore power.  Several road closures were reported, including Interstates 25 and 70.  A jackknifed semi-trailer backed up traffic for nearly 20 miles; on southbound I-25, between Denver and Colorado Springs.  In addition, a 50-ton boulder blocked the southbound lane of State Highway 285, near Parmalee Gulch.  Crews had to use explosives to break up the boulder and clear the debris.  Stranded buses and impassable roadways also forced several school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado moved west into lincoln county from Cheyenne County near Highway 287.

CZ_NAME: TELLER COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE ABOVE 7500 FT / PIKES PEAK BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moving along the Colorado/New Mexico border generated significant snow accumulations over the region.  The heavy wet snow combined with high winds...causing numerous power outages...downed power lines...and road closures.  Some of the heavier snow totals from this storm include...7 inches at Maysville in Chaffee County...12 to 14 inches in Westcliffe (Custer County)...Woodland Park (Teller County)...Sargents and Villa Grove (Saguache County). Sixteen to 20 inches of snow was measured near...Moffat (Alamosa County)...Silver Cliff (Custer County)...Monument (El Paso County) and Florissant (Teller County).  Also...snow drifts to around four feet were noted in northern El Paso County.  Nearly two feet of snow covered Black Forest (El Paso County)...and Crestone (Saguache County)...and Rosita (Custer County). In El Paso County...over 200 people were stranded...including 60 students from Miami-Yoder schools who were on a bus.  Thousands of people in eastern El Paso County were without power...some for several weeks.  Hundreds of electric transmission lines were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph buffeted the area, with local gusts up to 70 mph near Hotchkiss. In Montrose tree branches were downed. A tree branch fell on a power line near one home and caused a fire. Brief power outages occurred throughout Montrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moving along the Colorado/New Mexico border generated significant snow accumulations over the region.  The heavy wet snow combined with high winds...causing numerous power outages...downed power lines...and road closures.  Some of the heavier snow totals from this storm include...7 inches at Maysville in Chaffee County...12 to 14 inches in Westcliffe (Custer County)...Woodland Park (Teller County)...Sargents and Villa Grove (Saguache County). Sixteen to 20 inches of snow was measured near...Moffat (Alamosa County)...Silver Cliff (Custer County)...Monument (El Paso County) and Florissant (Teller County).  Also...snow drifts to around four feet were noted in northern El Paso County.  Nearly two feet of snow covered Black Forest (El Paso County)...and Crestone (Saguache County)...and Rosita (Custer County). In El Paso County...over 200 people were stranded...including 60 students from Miami-Yoder schools who were on a bus.  Thousands of people in eastern El Paso County were without power...some for several weeks.  Hundreds of electric transmission lines were downed.

Strong southwest winds buffeted eastern Utah and western Colorado ahead of a vigorous cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts from 45 to 63 mph buffeted the Grand Valley causing damage throughout the area. Near Fruita two women were blown over by a wind gust which led to a broken hip for one woman and a concussion for the other. Power poles and lines were downed in Grand Junction causing power and traffic light outages. Several trees and large tree branches were snapped off across the Grand Valley. Roof damage was reported in the Clifton area and a 50 foot tower at an agricultural research station in Orchard Mesa was blown over.

Severe thunderstorms, producing hail up to quarter size, and heavy rain; trained across northern portions of the Denver Metropolitan area.  The path of the storms stretched from around Golden to Denver International Airport.  The thunderstorms formed quickly following the passage of a cold front.  ||Some minor street flooding was also reported as hail clogged storm drains.  The hail piled up 4 inches deep at Elitch Gardens amusement park in downtown Denver.  Drivers were caught in up to 3 feet of water in some intersections.  Xcel reported scattered power failures around the Denver metropolitan area. At D.I.A. some brief delays occurred as airlines were forced to pull in their ground crews for safety reasons.  Lightning also struck two homes in Ft. Collins.  No one was hurt in either incident.  The incliment conditions forced the closure of Trail Ridge Road, in Rocky Mountain National Park, due to snow and very poor visibility.||Severe thunderstorms developed further south along the Palmer Divide. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter was reported over southern Elbert County.  Hail damage was reported in the towns of Elbert and Limon, where skylights and windows were broken.  Two tornadoes touched down but no structures were damaged.

An unusually strong storm system brought very strong winds to areas in the North Central Mountains, Front Range Foothills and Urban Corridor.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 55 mph at Denver International Airport to 101 mph at Carter Lake.  Peak wind gusts included:  101 mph at Carter Lake, 92 mph at Boulder, 85 mph, 2 miles southwest of Boulder, 83 mph, 10 miles south of Boulder, and 76 mph at Mines Peak and Pinewood Lake.  ||Hundreds of trees were downed in Frisco and across much of Summit County, destroying cars, damaging homes and businesses, and causing scattered outages across the county.  Approximately 700 acres of trees were downed across the county.  High winds forced the closure of Mt. Evans Road and Trail Ridge Road.  Several campgrounds had to be closed for tree removal.  In addition to the wind, some areas in the mountains accumulated from 4 to 6 inches of snow.    ||Several trees were uprooted across the Urban Corridor.  In Aurora, the driver of a car was injured when some building material blew off the Fitzsimmons Complex.  The debris landed on the car, knocking the driver unconscious.  The wind forced the cancellation of 60 flights at Denver International Airport.  Xcel reported outages in Boulder, Denver, Lakewood, Longmont and Windsor.

Long-term dryness and above normal temperatures caused abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions to prevail across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest monsoon brought surges of moisture to western Colorado resulting in periodic outbreaks of showers and thunderstorms. This resulted in some drought improvement across southwest Colorado, but abnormally dry conditions and water supply concerns persisted due to long-term dryness and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, producing hail up to quarter size, and heavy rain; trained across northern portions of the Denver Metropolitan area.  The path of the storms stretched from around Golden to Denver International Airport.  The thunderstorms formed quickly following the passage of a cold front.  ||Some minor street flooding was also reported as hail clogged storm drains.  The hail piled up 4 inches deep at Elitch Gardens amusement park in downtown Denver.  Drivers were caught in up to 3 feet of water in some intersections.  Xcel reported scattered power failures around the Denver metropolitan area. At D.I.A. some brief delays occurred as airlines were forced to pull in their ground crews for safety reasons.  Lightning also struck two homes in Ft. Collins.  No one was hurt in either incident.  The incliment conditions forced the closure of Trail Ridge Road, in Rocky Mountain National Park, due to snow and very poor visibility.||Severe thunderstorms developed further south along the Palmer Divide. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter was reported over southern Elbert County.  Hail damage was reported in the towns of Elbert and Limon, where skylights and windows were broken.  Two tornadoes touched down but no structures were damaged.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong storm system brought very strong winds to areas in the North Central Mountains, Front Range Foothills and Urban Corridor.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 55 mph at Denver International Airport to 101 mph at Carter Lake.  Peak wind gusts included:  101 mph at Carter Lake, 92 mph at Boulder, 85 mph, 2 miles southwest of Boulder, 83 mph, 10 miles south of Boulder, and 76 mph at Mines Peak and Pinewood Lake.  ||Hundreds of trees were downed in Frisco and across much of Summit County, destroying cars, damaging homes and businesses, and causing scattered outages across the county.  Approximately 700 acres of trees were downed across the county.  High winds forced the closure of Mt. Evans Road and Trail Ridge Road.  Several campgrounds had to be closed for tree removal.  In addition to the wind, some areas in the mountains accumulated from 4 to 6 inches of snow.    ||Several trees were uprooted across the Urban Corridor.  In Aurora, the driver of a car was injured when some building material blew off the Fitzsimmons Complex.  The debris landed on the car, knocking the driver unconscious.  The wind forced the cancellation of 60 flights at Denver International Airport.  Xcel reported outages in Boulder, Denver, Lakewood, Longmont and Windsor.

Thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in west central Chaffee County.  The hardest hit area was in and around Alpine.  Mud flows from the south slope of Mt. Princeton were up to six feet deep...causing damage and destruction of roads and houses.  People were evacuated to Buena Vista...and no one was seriously hurt.  Clean up took several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain saturated quartz monzonite soil...and the soil and boulders flowed down to the Chalk Creek Canyon floor and County Road 162.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms marched out of Wyoming and across the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  One tree was reportedly downed in Fort Collins.  A tornado reportedly touched down briefly near Ault.||Morgan County suffered the most damage as large hail, strong winds and very heavy rain moved through the area.  Utility pole were downed north of Brush causing scattered outages.  Two trees; 3 to 4 ft in diameter, were blown down near Wiggins and Brush.  Some memorial stones at the Cemetary in Brush were damaged.  Large hail the size of baseballs knocked the windows out of a home south of Brush.  Extensive crop damage was also reported in the same area.  Near State Highway 6 and Interstate 76; between Brush and Hillrose, hail piled up along the road with drifts up to a foot deep.  Plows from the Colorado Department of Transportation were sent out to clear the roads.  In addition, golfball hail caused extensive crop damage in western Logan County.||Heavy rain caused flash flooding in northeast Morgan County.  A bridge approach was washed out on County Road 31.  The wash was about 4 feet wide and 12 feet deep.  The highwater forced the closure of the roads to traffic.  There were other washouts along County Roads 26, 32, AA and JJ.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused minor flooding along the San Miguel County and Montrose County border. Rainfall reports in the area included 0.65 inches within 25 minutes and 0.63 inches within 15 minutes.  Estimated wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph accompanied the rainfall, with minor wind damage reported at a farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding and mudslides in west central and southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall over the Seven Castles formation of cliffs and gullies funneled down the Seven Castles Creek drainage and spilled into the Fryingpan River. The flood of water, rocks, and mud caused the course of the river channel to shift south. The debris field from the flood was about 1/4 mile wide and caused Eagle County road crews to scurry to the scene to clear up the debris covering the road. Debris ranged from knee-deep to waist-high. A temporary construction bridge and a section of Old Stage Road were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Rio Blanco River south of Pagosa Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding along the Rio Blanco River. Water up to 6 inches deep flowed across County Road 326. Big boulders were being moved by the water along with mud and other debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms marched out of Wyoming and across the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  One tree was reportedly downed in Fort Collins.  A tornado reportedly touched down briefly near Ault.||Morgan County suffered the most damage as large hail, strong winds and very heavy rain moved through the area.  Utility pole were downed north of Brush causing scattered outages.  Two trees; 3 to 4 ft in diameter, were blown down near Wiggins and Brush.  Some memorial stones at the Cemetary in Brush were damaged.  Large hail the size of baseballs knocked the windows out of a home south of Brush.  Extensive crop damage was also reported in the same area.  Near State Highway 6 and Interstate 76; between Brush and Hillrose, hail piled up along the road with drifts up to a foot deep.  Plows from the Colorado Department of Transportation were sent out to clear the roads. The wash was about 4 feet wide and 12 feet deep.  The highwater forced the closure of the roads to traffic.  There were other washouts along County Roads 26, 32, AA and JJ.

An influx of monsoonal moisture resulted in an outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud came down a drainage from the north and flowed across all lanes of Interstate 70 and continued across Highway 6. The flow of water and mud covered a 175 yard stretch of Interstate 70 and Highway 6 and was up to 4 feet deep when it struck the interstate. Two cars on Interstate 70 were stuck in 1.5 feet deep mud which was up to 3 feet deep in the west-bound lanes. The west-bound lanes of the interstate were closed for about 6 hours, while HIghway 6 and the east-bound lanes of the interstate were closed for a shorter period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in Sylvan Lake State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in the Sylvan Lake State Park area resulted in a flash flood. The flood came down from the east and was filled with debris that flowed up to 8 feet in depth down a normally dry drainage called Sneeve Gulch. Unofficial rainfall measurements of just over 2 inches came from the State Park Service. The flash flood widened the gulch from about 10 feet to 30 feet. The flash flood crossed USFS Road 400 and a Park Service tributary road. When the flash flood struck the main road, the culverts were immediately clogged with debris and the flow of water and debris went across the road, causing errosion to the road and depositing several feet of debris on the road. An 8 foot high berm adjacent to the Park Service tributary road was 10 feet in length prior to the flash flood. After the flood the berm was 70 feet in length. The debris within the flash flood included trees up to 2 feet in diameter and boulders up to 4 feet in diameter. About 80 people were trapped at the Sylvan Lake State Park campground that night. The flash flood came close to a couple of State Park structures. Otherwise, there was no significant property damage except to the USFS Road 400 where the culverts needed to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter.  The most severe weather occurred over southeastern Douglas and southwestern Elbert County, from around Larkspur to Elbert.  Large hail in this location caused extensive damage to vehicles.  A tornado was also observed, but it did no damage.  In Parker, lightning struck a home, causing extensive damage.  The bolt entered the house's electrical system and started a fire when sparks from an overloaded outlet fell into a nearby trash can.  The ensuing fire damaged the attic and top floor.  The strike caused $100,000 in damage and rendered the home uninhabitable for the next 3 months.

An outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and debris flows in northwest and west central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a wildfire burn area resulted in flash flooding and debris flows which began on higher terrain to the east of County Road 137. Water and debris flowed out of 3 separate drainages and ran across County Road 137. One flow was 4 feet deep across the road, while the other two flows were 1 to 2 feet deep where they crossed the road.  Total debris deposits spread out over a 1.5 mile section of the road. Several vehicles got stuck trying to drive through it. One house became completely surrounded by debris, and a garage was filled with mud and rock, covering an ATV completely and up to the wheel wells of a pickup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and debris flows in northwest and west central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .75 inches of rain in 30 minutes resulting in 1 inch of water over Highway 135.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and debris flows in northwest and west central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in 2 to 3 inches of water to flow over County Road 45.5 at Shire Gulch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and debris flows in northwest and west central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a wildfire burn area resulted in flash flooding and debris flows. Large amounts of debris, including partially burned trees, cut trees, mud and boulders were carried down drainages to the west of County Road 137. The debris spread out across Highway 6 to a depth of about 2 feet over a span of 40 to 50 feet. Debris was also deposited across railroad tracks along the Vulcan Ditch which was totally clogged with debris. The flow then spread out across Interstate 70 and covered a 300 yard stretch of both the east and west bound lanes. The interstate was closed for about 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced damaging winds at the Browns Park National Wildlife Refuge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches were broken off at Brown's Park National Wildlife Refuge. A wind gust of 59 mph was measured at the Ladore RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and debris flows in northwest and west central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain producing thunderstorms caused water and mud to inundate an irrigation canal and then flow across Interstate 70. Spotter and public reports indicated between 2.5 and 3 inches of rainfall occurred over a period ranging from 15 to 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down across 24 1/2 Road between F 1/2 Road and G Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding near Ft. Lupton.  Up to 8 inches of water was reported across County Road 18.  In addition, several other county roads in the immediate area were washed out.

An influx of monsoonal moisture resulted in an outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused water and debris to flow down a canyon north of Interstate 70 and onto the interstate near mile marker 154. The flow was about 40 feet wide and 2 feet deep across the westbound lanes of the interstate. The flow contained a considerable amount of rocks, mud, and other debris that was deposited onto the road. The westbound lanes were closed for about 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of monsoonal moisture resulted in an outbreak of heavy rain producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flash flooding across a forest service road between Highway 133 and the Avalanche Creek Campground. About 3 feet of debris was deposited on the road in the wake of the flash flood. Dozens of campers were stranded in the campground until the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flash flooding southwest of Aspen in a recreational day-use area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused water and debris to flow down a normally dry drainage running through the Maroon Creek Day Use Area provided by the USFS. The water and debris average depth was 5 feet with a width of 30 feet. The flash flood hit the Maroon Creek Road and a trailhead parking lot where it spread out about 100 feet wide, clogging culverts and burying the road and day-use area under 1 to 2 feet of debris. Improved trails were destroyed and tons of debris had to be removed from the road and day-use area. Additionally, hailstones up to 1/2 inch diameter accompanied the heavy rain. There were people camping nearby in tents when the event occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flash flooding along Highway 65.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused water and mud 6 inches deep to flow across Highway 65. Boulders the size of volleyballs were deposited along the road as well.

Heavy rainfall concentrated just north of the La Plata and San Juan County line produced a massive flow of water, rock, and mud which flowed across the Durango to Silverton Narrow Gage Railroad tracks and into the Animas River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy convective rainfall concentrated just north of the La Plata and San Juan County line produced a massive flow of water, rock, and mud which traveled across the Durango to Silverton Narrow Gage Railroad track and dammed up the Animas River about one mile north of Needleton. Initially, the entire river channel was dammed.  The river bed at that location was raised 8 feet on the east side of the channel and remained blocked on the west side of the channel. The debris flow covered about 250 feet of railroad track. The peak depth of debris on the railroad track was measured at close to 45 feet. The railroad track did not sustain significant damage, because the lowest several feet of debris on the track consisted primarily of mud. On top of the mud was a lot of rock material with some trees mixed in it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, producing hail up to quarter size, and heavy rain; trained across northern portions of the Denver Metropolitan area.  The path of the storms stretched from around Golden to Denver International Airport.  The thunderstorms formed quickly following the passage of a cold front.  ||Some minor street flooding was also reported as hail clogged storm drains.  The hail piled up 4 inches deep at Elitch Gardens amusement park in downtown Denver.  Drivers were caught in up to 3 feet of water in some intersections.  Xcel reported scattered power failures around the Denver metropolitan area. At D.I.A. some brief delays occurred as airlines were forced to pull in their ground crews for safety reasons.  Lightning also struck two homes in Ft. Collins.  No one was hurt in either incident.  The incliment conditions forced the closure of Trail Ridge Road, in Rocky Mountain National Park, due to snow and very poor visibility.||Severe thunderstorms developed further south along the Palmer Divide. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter was reported over southern Elbert County.  Hail damage was reported in the towns of Elbert and Limon, where skylights and windows were broken.  Two tornadoes touched down but no structures were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system allowed for heavy rain to fall over parts of Northeastern Colorado, affecting primarily Sedgwick and Phillips Counties. Severe thunderstorms produce large hail and very heavy rain.  Downed power lines were reported over parts of Sedgwick County on the 11th.  Farmers in the area reported extensive flooding over southeastern Sedwick County from around Sixteen-mile Corner east to the Nebraska state line.  Several basements were also flooded. ||In addition, extensive flooding and flash flooding forced dozens of road closures in northeast Phillips County.  Colorado Highway 23; east of Amherst, and U.S. Highway 6; east of Holyoke, were closed due to flooding.  Dozens of secondary roads were also reportedly under water.  A section of railroad track about 1.5 miles southwest of Amherst was washed out with up to 10 feet of water covering the highway.  Extensive field flooding was also observed.  In a two day period, a trained spotter in eastern Phillips County measured over 10.3 inches of rainfall.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding in and near the towns of Brush, Fort Morgan and Wiggins.  In Fort Morgan, extensive street flooding stalled several vehicles in standing water.  The floodwaters also spread into nearby businesses; the water ran across the floors and collected in the basements.  Customers in those businesses were left stranded until the water safely subsided.  In addition, some mulch and landscaping in the area was washed away.  Several county roads around Fort Morgan were inundated with up to 8 inches of water.  In Brush and Wiggins, extensive street and basement flooding was reported along with a number of minor washouts.

Heavy thunderstorm rains of up to 3 inches produced severe mud flows over sections of county roads 162 and 292 in Chalk Creek Canyon.  The Alpine subdivision was in the path of the worst mudflow, where mandatory evacuations occurred.  More than 30 houses and over 100 people were evacuated, as mud and boulders as deep as 8 feet moved down the mountain side.  At least 15 houses were damaged, along with landscaping and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains of up to 3 inches in a short period of time produced severe mudslides in the Alpine area.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought up to 15 inches of snow over sections of southern Colorado.  Seven inches of snow was noted at Black Forest, southwest of downtown Colorado Springs, north of Florissant in Teller County and northwest of Aguilar, along the Huerfano-Las Animas county line.  Nine to 15 inches of snow occurred in and around  Beulah, Rye, San Isabel, Silver Cliff, Rosita, and Wetmore in western Pueblo and Custer Counties.  Twelve inches of snow was measured around Woodland Park in Teller county. Due to the time frame of the snow event, snow advisories covered the event.  Meantime, heavy snow accumulations over 10 inches occurred over the eastern Sawatch Range and Mosquito Range, covered by a winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought moderate to heavy snowfall to the North-Central Mountains as well as to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Storm totals included:  11.5 inches at the Tower Snotel, 8 inches; 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs, 7.5 inches; 3 miles west of Jamestown, 7 inches; 7 miles east-northeast of Red Feather Lakes at Roach Snotel, with 6 inches observed at Bear Lake National Park, 1 mile north of Bergen Park, 3 miles southwest of Conifer, Pinewood Lake and Winter Park.  Along the Urban Corridor there were some locally higher amounts reported near the foothills of Boulder and Larimer Counties.  Storm totals in those areas included:  7 inches; 3 miles south-southeast of Fort Collins with 6 inches observed in Boulder and at Horsetooth Inlet Bay.  Elsewhere across the Urban Corridor, storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

A strong cold front pushed through the region resulting in northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph during the day.  Several wind gusts of 58 to 61 mph were reported over eastern CO, western KS and southwestern NE during the morning and early afternoon hours.  No known damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust at Burlington airport ASOS, 4 miles south of Burlington, CO.
